title: Ensure 'external scripts enabled' Database Flag for Cloud  SQL SQL Server Instance Is Set to 'off'

input_row.title: Ensure 'external scripts enabled' Database Flag for Cloud  SQL SQL Server Instance Is Set to 'off'
input_row.description: It is recommended to set external scripts enabled database flag for Cloud SQL SQL Server instance to off
input_row.rationale: external scripts enabled enable the execution of scripts with certain remote language extensions. This property is OFF by default. When Advanced Analytics Services is installed, setup can optionally set this property to true. As the External Scripts Enabled feature allows scripts external to SQL such as files located in an R library to be executed, which could adversely affect the security of the system, hence this should be disabled. This recommendation is applicable to SQL Server database instances.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the instance to open its Instance Overview . Ensure the database flag external scripts enabled that has been set is listed under the Database flags section. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Ensure the below command returns off for every Cloud SQL SQL Server database instance gcloud sql instances describe --format=json | jq '.settings.databaseFlags[] | select(.name=="external scripts enabled")|.value' In the output, database flags are listed under the settings as the collection databaseFlags.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the SQL Server instance for which you want to enable to database flag. 3. Click Edit. 4. Scroll down to the Flags section. 5. To set a flag that has not been set on the instance before, click Add item, choose the flag external scripts enabled from the drop-down menu, and set its value to off. 6. Click Save to save your changes. 7. Confirm your changes under Flags on the Overview page. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Configure the external scripts enabled database flag for every Cloud SQL SQL Server database instance using the below command. gcloud sql instances patch --database-flags "external scripts enabled"=off Note: This command will overwrite all database flags previously set. To keep those and add new ones, include the values for all flags you want set on the instance; any flag not specifically included is set to its default value. For flags that do not take a value, specify the flag name followed by an equals sign ("=").
input_row.impact: Setting custom flags via command line on certain instances will cause all omitted flags to be reset to defaults. This may cause you to lose custom flags and could result in unforeseen complications or instance restarts. Because of this, it is recommended you apply these flags changes during a period of low usage.
input_row.default_value: By default external scripts enabled is off
input_row.additional_information: WARNING: This patch modifies database flag values, which may require your instance to be restarted. Check the list of supported flags - https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/sqlserver/flags - to see if your instance will be restarted when this patch is submitted. Note: some database flag settings can affect instance availability or stability, and remove the instance from the Cloud SQL SLA. For information about these flags, see Operational Guidelines." Note: Configuring the above flag restarts the Cloud SQL instance.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit can be fully verified using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK with the indicated `gcloud` command that checks the specific database flag value.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_sql_instance_external_scripts_enabled_set_off

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
- Use the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command `gcloud sql instances describe --format=json` to retrieve detailed information about the Cloud SQL SQL Server instances.
- From the output, focus on the `settings.databaseFlags` property to check if `external scripts enabled` flag is listed, and confirm that its value is set to `off`.
- The specific GCP API call can be the `sql.instances.get` method to get the instance settings programmatically.
- Validate the configuration property by ensuring that:
  1. The `external scripts enabled` flag exists.
  2. The value of this flag is equal to `off`.

**Clear Pass/Fail criteria:**
- Pass: The `external scripts enabled` flag is present and its value is `off`.
- Fail: The `external scripts enabled` flag is either not present, or its value is not `off`. 

This automated check ensures compliance with the CIS control by programmatically verifying the security setting for the Cloud SQL SQL Server instance.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The compliance check can be effectively performed using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud), which allows for programmatic access to Cloud SQL instance configurations.
- The use of the `gcloud sql instances describe` command is appropriate for retrieving the necessary information about the database flags, specifically the `external scripts enabled` flag.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9

- The automation details are technically accurate. The `gcloud sql instances describe --format=json` command is valid and correctly identifies the method to check the `settings.databaseFlags` property.
- The use of the `sql.instances.get` API method is also correct for programmatically accessing the instance settings.
- The program name `gcp_sql_instance_external_scripts_enabled_set_off` is descriptive and follows the convention, but it could be slightly improved for clarity (see Improvements section).

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Name:** Consider renaming the program to `gcp_sql_server_external_scripts_disabled` to more clearly indicate that the check is specific to SQL Server instances and that the flag should be disabled.
- **Additional Considerations:** Ensure that the audit script accounts for cases where the `external scripts enabled` flag might not be explicitly set, as the default behavior should be considered. If the flag is not present, it should be treated as a fail unless the default is known to be `off`.
- **Alternative Methods:** While the current methods are sufficient, consider using GCP Config for continuous monitoring and compliance checks, which can provide additional automation and alerting capabilities.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded and uses appropriate GCP tools and methods to automate the compliance check. The instructions and criteria are clear and actionable.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- Modify the program name to `gcp_sql_server_external_scripts_disabled` for improved clarity.
- Ensure the audit script handles cases where the flag is not explicitly set, considering the default behavior of the SQL Server instance.
- Consider integrating GCP Config for ongoing compliance monitoring if not already in place.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** Both AI opinions agree that the compliance check can be automated using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) and GCP APIs. The `gcloud sql instances describe --format=json` command allows for programmatic access to the Cloud SQL instance configurations, specifically to verify the `external scripts enabled` flag. This approach is technically feasible and aligns with the capabilities of GCP APIs to retrieve and validate the necessary configuration settings.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_sql_server_external_scripts_disabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **GCP Resource/Service:** Cloud SQL SQL Server Instances
- **API/CLI Method:** Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) `sql instances describe --format=json`
- **Properties to Check:** `settings.databaseFlags` property, specifically the `external scripts enabled` flag
- **Pass Criteria:** The `external scripts enabled` flag is present and its value is `off`.
- **Fail Criteria:** The `external scripts enabled` flag is either not present, or its value is not `off`.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** There were no disagreements between the AI opinions; both supported the automated approach. The decision to automate is based on the technical feasibility of using GCP APIs to perform the compliance check.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Google_Cloud_Platform_Foundation_Benchmark_v4_0_0
